name: Eddie Harris
abstract: Eddie Harris (October 20, 1934 – November 5, 1996) was an American jazz musician, best known for playing tenor saxophone and for introducing the electrically amplified saxophone. He was also fluent on the electric piano and organ.

about_quotes.speaker: Steve Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: he’s probably been one of the biggest influences on me, one of the first people, well probably the first guy that I idolized so to speak, even before I could even pick up a saxophone
about_quotes.doc_title: Steve Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 25, 2002

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: I got the first opportunity to travel with Eddie Harris. He had Harold Jones was his drummer and Harold was going to music school. He had some finals that he had to be around for so I subbed for Harold.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Ellis Marsalis
about_quotes.evidence: There is one recorded example of me doing what might be called free jazz and that was with Eddie Harris you know. It was called “Homecoming.” It’s a duo, just the two of us
about_quotes.doc_title: Ellis Marsalis interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Orleans, Louisiana, January 7, 2020

about_quotes.speaker: Joel Dorn
about_quotes.evidence: I worked with Mose © Fillius Jazz Archive -5- Allison, you know I worked with Herbie Mann a little bit and then Les McCann and then Eddie Harris.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joel Dorn interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Lanny Morgan
about_quotes.evidence: Along with myself it was Eddie Harris and Leo Wright and Don Ellis and Cedar Walton. So it was really playing with good musicians
about_quotes.doc_title: Lanny Morgan interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 14, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Al Gafa
about_quotes.evidence: I think he was a more influential on my career than Johnny Smith... So I loved him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Al Gafa interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, May 2, 2023
